stoic_text:
Thus we do not have any original writings of Epictetus. Like G. H. Mead
in recent times, he was completely dedicated to the human and
intellectual problems of his students. He left it for them to preserve
what they considered to be the lasting message of the teacher. In
contrast to Seneca and Marcus Aurelius, Epictetus had no subjective
approach to the Stoic doctrines. Moral philosophy was the center of his
teaching, and epistemology was only instrumental. It is even permissible
to say that he took physics or cosmology too lightly. If this is granted,
we must admit that he is completely absorbed by the fundamentals of Stoic
thought as presented in the _Enchiridion_. Epictetus’ personality is
totally integrated in the act of reasoning which establishes conformity
with nature.
neutral_text:
I notice this passage isn't actually from Epictetus himself - it's a modern scholarly analysis *about* Epictetus and his teaching approach. It's discussing his biography and philosophical method rather than giving philosophical advice.

Since this is academic commentary rather than a philosophical teaching passage, I can't rewrite it as "modern conventional advice about the SAME topic" because the topic here is historical/biographical information about an ancient philosopher.

Could you provide an actual passage from Epictetus's teachings (from the *Discourses* or *Enchiridion*) that contains philosophical advice? That would allow me to follow your instructions properly - taking his advice on a topic and rewriting it with modern mainstream reasoning instead of Stoic philosophy.

stoic_text:
Remember that it is not he who gives abuse or blows, who affronts, but
the view we take of these things as insulting. When, therefore, anyone
provokes you, be assured that it is your own opinion which provokes you.
Try, therefore, in the first place, not to be bewildered by appearances.
For if you once gain time and respite, you will more easily command
yourself.
neutral_text:
Remember that getting upset isn't really about what someone says or does to you—it's about how you choose to interpret their actions. When someone tries to get under your skin, recognize that you're giving them power over your emotions by taking the bait.

So when someone pushes your buttons, take a step back before reacting. Don't let your first emotional response drive the bus. If you can pause and give yourself a moment to think it through, you'll be way better at responding in a way that actually serves your interests instead of just making the situation worse. Most of the time, staying calm and collected puts you in the driver's seat.

stoic_text:
When anyone shows himself vain on being able to understand and interpret
the works of Chrysippus,[7] say to yourself: “Unless Chrysippus had
written obscurely, this person would have had nothing to be vain of. But
what do I desire? To understand nature, and follow her. I ask, then, who
interprets her; and hearing that Chrysippus does, I have recourse to him.
I do not understand his writings. I seek, therefore, one to interpret
_them_.” So far there is nothing to value myself upon. And when I find an
interpreter, what remains is to make use of his instructions. This alone
is the valuable thing. But if I admire merely the interpretation, what do
I become more than a grammarian, instead of a philosopher, except,
indeed, that instead of Homer I interpret Chrysippus? When anyone,
therefore, desires me to read Chrysippus to him, I rather blush when I
cannot exhibit actions that are harmonious and consonant with his
discourse.
neutral_text:
When someone brags about understanding complex academic theories or being able to explain difficult texts, remember this: they're just showing off their ability to decode confusing writing. Ask yourself what you're really after. You want practical knowledge that actually improves your life, right? So if some expert has useful insights, great - find someone who can break it down for you in plain English. There's no shame in needing an explanation.

But here's the key: once you get the explanation, you need to actually apply what you've learned. That's where the real value is. If you just become someone who's good at explaining theories to other people, you're basically a human Wikipedia - you can recite information but you're not actually living any better. When someone asks me to explain some self-help concept or life philosophy to them, I honestly feel embarrassed if I can't point to concrete ways I've actually used that advice to improve my own situation. Talk is cheap; results matter.

stoic_text:
[5][This refers to an anecdote given in full by Simplicius, in his
   commentary on this passage, of a man assaulted and killed on his way
   to consult the oracle, while his companion, deserting him, took refuge
   in the temple till cast out by the Deity.—Tr.]

stoic_text:
[7][Chrysippus (_c._ 280-207 B.C.) was a Stoic philosopher who became
   head of the Stoa after Cleanthes. His works, which are lost, were most
   influential and were generally accepted as the authoritative
   interpretation of orthodox Stoic philosophy.—Ed.]
